skill.name: gen-paylink-govilo
skill.description: Upload a ZIP, folder, or selected files to Govilo and create a paid unlock link through the Govilo Bot API.
skill.summary: Create Govilo paid unlock links from local files using a CLI workflow.
skill.author: hau823823

security_audit.summary: The static analyzer correctly identified network access, environment credential handling, and filesystem packaging as meaningful risks for this community skill. I found no prompt injection text or confirmed malicious intent, but the skill can upload arbitrary user-selected files to an external service using a bearer API key and should not be published without strong warnings and review. Markdown backtick detections and weak-cryptography detections are mostly false positives from documentation formatting, API key examples, and ZIP compression.

high_findings.title: External Upload of User-Selected Files
high_findings.description: The workflow packages local input paths and uploads the resulting ZIP to Govilo storage through a presigned URL. This is core functionality, but it creates a high data disclosure risk if users select sensitive project folders or private files.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code directly packages user-provided paths and performs an HTTP PUT to the upload URL. The behavior is intentional, but the privacy impact is clear.

high_findings.title: Bearer API Key Used for External Service Calls
high_findings.description: The skill reads GOVILO_API_KEY from the environment and places it in an Authorization header for Govilo API requests. This is expected for the integration, but any misuse or unexpected endpoint change would expose a sensitive token.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: Environment credential access and bearer header construction are explicit. I found no evidence that the key is sent outside the configured Govilo API base URL.

medium_findings.title: Insecure Temporary Archive Path Creation
medium_findings.description: The packager uses tempfile.mktemp to choose ZIP paths before writing archives. This can create a local race condition and should be replaced with NamedTemporaryFile or mkstemp.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: tempfile.mktemp is directly used for files that are later opened for writing. This is a known unsafe temporary-file pattern.

medium_findings.title: Recursive Directory Packaging Has No Secret Exclusions
medium_findings.description: When a directory is provided, the code recursively includes all files under that directory. It does not exclude hidden files, environment files, keys, Git metadata, or other common secret locations.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The recursive rglob behavior is visible, and the documentation warns about dedicated env files. The code itself does not enforce those safety boundaries.

medium_findings.title: Installer Documentation Pipes Remote Scripts to Shell
medium_findings.description: The setup guide recommends shell and PowerShell install commands that execute remote installer content. This is documentation rather than skill runtime behavior, but it is still a supply-chain risk for users who follow it.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are explicit setup instructions. They point to a common uv installer source, so this is risky guidance rather than evidence of malicious intent.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backtick Command Findings Are False Positives
low_findings.description: Many external command findings come from markdown code formatting and CLI examples. I found no Ruby backtick execution in the Python scripts.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged regions are documentation snippets or inline code spans, not executable Ruby code. The executable files are Python and do not use shell backticks.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Findings Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The flagged weak-cryptography patterns appear to match API key examples, numeric API error codes, ZIP compression constants, and payment values. I found no evidence of MD5, SHA-1, or custom cryptography.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review did not identify cryptographic algorithm usage in the flagged files. The matches are semantically unrelated to cryptography.

low_findings.title: No Prompt Injection Attempt Found
low_findings.description: I checked the reviewed skill files for suspicious override language and found no evidence of instructions that attempt to bypass audit methodology.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Targeted search found no common prompt-injection phrases. This does not prove absence across every possible wording, but no evidence was found in reviewed files.

dangerous_patterns.title: Network Upload With Local File Input
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill combines local filesystem reads, ZIP packaging, and HTTP upload. This pattern is sensitive because user-selected files leave the local machine.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The file-to-network flow is explicit in the workflow and API client. It matches the skill purpose but remains a high-risk pattern.

dangerous_patterns.title: Environment Secret Used in Authorization Header
dangerous_patterns.description: The integration reads a secret environment variable and uses it as a bearer token for API calls.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The exact secret name and Authorization header construction are present in code. This is necessary for the service but security-sensitive.

dangerous_patterns.title: Unsafe Temporary File Name Generation
dangerous_patterns.description: Temporary ZIP archives are created using a filename returned by mktemp, which is vulnerable to local race conditions.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The unsafe API is directly visible at both archive creation sites. No mitigating exclusive-open behavior is present.

content.value_statement: Creators need a fast way to sell access to small digital files. This skill packages files, uploads them to Govilo, and returns a paid unlock link.
actual_capabilities: Creates Govilo unlock links from a ZIP file, folder, or selected files.
Packages non-ZIP inputs into a ZIP archive before upload.
Uses GOVILO_API_KEY and SELLER_ADDRESS to call the Govilo Bot API.
Requests a presigned upload URL and uploads the ZIP to Govilo storage.
Returns the unlock URL, item ID, file count, and total upload size.
Provides setup guidance for uv, Govilo API keys, and Base wallet addresses.
limitations: Requires a valid Govilo account, API key, and seller wallet address.
Only supports ZIP uploads up to 20 MB and up to 20 files.
Uploads selected content to an external Govilo service.
Does not automatically exclude secrets or hidden files from selected folders.

use_cases.title: Sell a Small Digital Download
use_cases.description: Package a finished ZIP and create a Govilo unlock link for customer purchases.

use_cases.title: Automate Product Link Creation
use_cases.description: Turn repeated file upload and pay link creation into a guided CLI workflow.

use_cases.title: Test Govilo Bot API Workflows
use_cases.description: Validate presign, upload, and item creation steps from a local development environment.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Basic Link
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner setup with one ZIP file.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use gen-paylink-govilo to create a Govilo unlock link for this ZIP. Ask me for the title, price, and seller address first.

prompt_templates.title: Prepare a Folder Upload
prompt_templates.scenario: Package a folder into a Govilo upload.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a Govilo pay link from this folder. Check the file count and warn me before uploading anything sensitive.

prompt_templates.title: Use Existing Environment Credentials
prompt_templates.scenario: Repeat use with a dedicated Govilo env file.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a Govilo unlock link using my dedicated Govilo environment file. Use this title, price, and description.

prompt_templates.title: Review Before Publishing
prompt_templates.prompt: Inspect the selected files, confirm they meet Govilo limits, then create a paid unlock link only after I approve the upload.

output_examples.input: A creator asks for a pay link for a 12 MB ZIP product.
output_examples.output: The assistant confirms the product title, price, description, and seller address, then provides the Govilo unlock link after upload.

output_examples.input: A developer provides a folder with more than 20 files.
output_examples.output: The assistant reports that the folder exceeds the supported file limit and asks for a smaller ZIP or reduced file set.

output_examples.input: A user tries to run the workflow without a Govilo API key.
output_examples.output: The assistant explains that a Govilo API key is required and points the user to the setup guide.

best_practices: Use a dedicated Govilo environment file that contains only Govilo credentials.
Review every selected file before upload, especially when uploading a folder.
Use ZIP inputs when possible to control exactly what is published.
anti_patterns: Do not point the workflow at a shared project folder without reviewing its contents.
Do not use a shared .env file that contains unrelated service secrets.
Do not upload private customer data, keys, or unreleased work unless Govilo storage is approved for it.

faq.question: What does this skill create?
faq.answer: It creates a Govilo paid unlock link for uploaded digital files.

faq.question: Which credentials are required?
faq.answer: It requires GOVILO_API_KEY and a seller address on Base chain.

faq.question: Can it upload a folder?
faq.answer: Yes. It recursively packages files in the selected folder before upload.

faq.question: What are the upload limits?
faq.answer: The ZIP must be 20 MB or smaller and contain no more than 20 files.

faq.question: Does it keep files local?
faq.answer: No. The workflow uploads selected files to Govilo storage through a presigned URL.

faq.question: Is it safe to use with project folders?
faq.answer: Use caution. The code does not automatically exclude secrets, hidden files, or environment files.
